title: Enforce WebGL-only rendering backend
summary: The codebase already uses Three.js WebGLRenderer and PerspectiveCamera exclusively with no Canvas2D code present. The primary gap is AC4: there is no explicit WebGL availability check with a graceful fallback message when WebGL is unavailable. The work is minimal — add a WebGL feature gate before renderer creation and a user-facing fallback notice.

currentState.gracefulFallback: Missing — no explicit WebGL detection; Three.js will throw an uncaught error if WebGL is unavailable

steps.title: Add WebGL availability detection to scene bootstrap
steps.description: Add a WebGL feature check at the top of initScene(). Test for WebGL support by creating a temporary canvas and requesting a 'webgl' or 'webgl2' context. If neither is available, throw a typed error (e.g., WebGLUnavailableError) that callers can catch.
details: Create a helper function detectWebGL(): boolean that tests for WebGLRenderingContext and attempts getContext('webgl2') || getContext('webgl')
Call it at the start of initScene()
If false, throw a custom WebGLUnavailableError with a descriptive message
Export the error class so callers can instanceof-check it

steps.title: Add graceful fallback UI in the entry point
steps.description: Wrap the initScene() call in a try/catch. If a WebGLUnavailableError is caught, display a minimal full-screen dark message informing the visitor that their browser does not support WebGL and the experience cannot be rendered. Do not attempt Canvas2D rendering.
details: Render a styled <div> into the app container with a short message (e.g., 'This experience requires WebGL, which is not available in your browser.')
Style it to match the dark aesthetic (black background, light text, centered)
Return early — do not start the render loop, audio, or any other systems
Re-throw any other errors so they are not silently swallowed

steps.title: Add compile-time guard against Canvas2D imports
steps.description: Add a brief code comment at the renderer creation site documenting that Canvas2D is intentionally excluded per US-039 and that WebGLRenderer is the only supported backend. This serves as a human-readable guard for future contributors.
details: Single-line comment above the WebGLRenderer constructor: '// US-039: WebGL is the only rendering backend; Canvas2D is not supported'

steps.title: Verify acceptance criteria
steps.description: Run build and manually verify all four acceptance criteria are met.
details: Grep codebase for CanvasRenderingContext2D / getContext('2d') — expect zero hits in src/
Confirm WebGLRenderer is the only renderer instantiated
Confirm PerspectiveCamera is in the scene graph
Simulate WebGL unavailability (e.g., override getContext to return null) and confirm the fallback message renders without console errors

risksAndConsiderations: The WebGL detection check adds a trivial amount of startup cost (one throwaway canvas context creation). This is negligible.
Three.js internally checks for WebGL but throws a generic error — the custom check gives us control over the user-facing message.
Context loss handling already exists in scene.ts for runtime GPU crashes; the new check only covers initial availability.

acceptanceCriteriaMapping.AC2 — Canvas2D is not used as the primar: Already satisfied (no Canvas2D in codebase). Step 3 adds documentation. Step 4 verifies.
acceptanceCriteriaMapping.AC4 — Rendering fails gracefully only if: Not yet satisfied. Steps 1-2 implement the graceful fallback.
